sentences.grammarNote: 暇 (hima) means 'free time / unoccupied'. In Japanese, asking 'are you free?' is literally 'are you hima?'. The topic marker は is often dropped in casual speech: 今週末暇？

sentences.base_language: I want to see a movie. Do you want to come?
sentences.target_language: 映画を見たいです。一緒に来ませんか？
sentences.romanized: Eiga wo mitai desu. Issho ni kimasen ka?
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ませんか is a polite invitation form: 'won't you do…?' It's softer and more inviting than a direct request. Compare 来てください (please come) with 来ませんか (won't you come?).

sentences.base_language: What time does the movie start?
sentences.target_language: 映画は何時に始まりますか？
sentences.romanized: Eiga wa nanji ni hajimarimasu ka?

sentences.grammarNote: 半 (han) means 'half past' when used after an hour: 七時半 = 7:30. For time in Japanese, the particle に marks a specific point in time: 七時に (at 7 o'clock).

sentences.target_language: どこで待ち合わせましょうか？
入口で待ち合わせましょう。
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ましょう is the volitional form of polite verbs: 'let's do…'. 待ち合わせる (machiawaseru) specifically means 'to meet up by arrangement', more precise than just 会う (au, to meet).

sentences.target_language: 遅刻しないでください！
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ないでください is the standard negative request: 'please don't do…'. 遅刻する (chikoku suru) = 'to be late / to arrive late'. In casual speech this shortens to 遅刻しないで！
